Procedure upon filing of the petition shall be as in other civil cases. C. Upon filing of the petition, the court shall issue an order requiring the respondent to appear at the time and place set for the hearing on the petition. Service of the order and a copy of the petition shall be as provided in the rules of civil procedure. [emphasis added] As set forth in that statute the venue of the court in both "proceedings to establish or enforce" support duties may be brought in the county of residence of either party or of the children. texts.full_text: Frank and John Adams, whose “Shaker Loops” she conducted here, and whose work she has conducted for other orchestras. Jim Kleinman, the symphony’s executive director and founder of the “Under construction” program, said, “She and I have talked quite a bit during her work here, and since, and she’s enthusiastic about the longtime, deeper investment we’re making in Bay Area composers.” Carneiro said of the program, “I haven’t seen many programs like that; none exactly like it—how sustained, systematic, with the kind of feedback between the orchestra, conductor, composer and audience. The music director in the U.S. is a bridge between the orchestra and the community. I want to find ways of interacting with the community, not only through programmed concerts.” Kleinman looked back on the search process, which began with Nagano’s announcement to the musicians, then the public, that he’d be stepping down: “That was Jan. 19, 2007, so it’s been two years, almost to the date. I’d been on board less than two months, so supporting Kent’s announcement and working on the search process was one of my first main tasks. Within a week, the orchestra had elected three musicians to the search committee of 10, which then met face to face. It was an amazing process; from the outside, it looked quick, but ironically for those of us living through it, it seemed a long time.” Committee member Deborah Shidler, principal oboeist of the orchestra, commented on the Search Committee’s task: “We didn’t have a lot of time, but guided by Jim and Board President and Committee Chair Kathleen Henschel, we were able to go quickly, get into the nitty gritty ... There was the chance we’d make the decision, and whoever was selected wouldn’t be available, but Joana’s going to be there for all four concerts from October.” Shidler continued, “The committee paid a lot of attention to the musicians and to the online surveys. A: The easiest way to get a file onto the class path is to put it into src/main/resources. src/dist is for adding files to the distribution (i.e. zip file), not to the Jar/class path. A: This isn't a particularly good answer to your question, but I've found a few instances where the startScripts task doesn't quite yet have the flexibility we need. I've worked around a couple of these instances by editing the file contents directly... not exactly taking advantage of Gradle's great model and not particularly recommended, but at least shows off how flexible Gradle is! So you could hack something into the classpath like this: tasks.startScripts { doLast { def scriptFile = file "${outputDir}/${applicationName}" scriptFile.text = scriptFile.text.replace('CLASSPATH=$APP_HOME/lib', 'CLASSPATH=$APP_HOME/conf/:$APP_HOME/lib') } } Be aware of breaking the start

texts.full_text: the Conference of the Parties (COP15) held in December 2009 in Copenhagen developed countries pledged to provide new and additional resources, including forestry and investments, approaching USD 30 billion for the period 2010 - 2012 and with balanced allocation between mitigation and adaptation. This collective commitment has come to be known as "Fast-start Finance" (FSF). Following up on this pledge, the COP in Cancun, in December of December 2009 agreed that developed countries should provide a further USD $100 billion per year by 2020. This is intended to support developing nations' efforts at both mitigation and adaptation. The Fast-start Finance was meant as an immediate injection into the global climate effort, with the understanding it would be followed up on in subsequent years until reaching this higher annual target of US$30-$50 billion for developing countries by 2013-2025. The Green Climate Fund (GCF) was established as a key component to manage and distribute these contributions. texts.full_text: mobility spectrometry - the impact of fluorescent labelling. The analysis of complex oligosaccharides is traditionally based on multidimensional workflows where liquid chromatography is coupled to tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). Due to the presence of multiple isomers, which cannot be distinguished easily using tandem MS, a detailed structural elucidation is still challenging in many cases. Recently, ion mobility spectrometry (IMS) showed great potential as an additional structural parameter in glycan analysis. While the time-scale of the IMS separation is fully compatible to that of LC-MS-based workflows, there are very few reports in which both techniques have been directly coupled for glycan analysis. As a result, there is little knowledge on how the derivatization with fluorescent labels as common in glycan LC-MS affects the mobility and, as a result, the selectivity of IMS separations. Here, we address this problem by systemically investigating the impact of fluorescent labelling on the ion mobility separation of isomeric glycans. We employed several commonly used fluorescent labels, including 2-aminobenzamide (2-AB), 2-aminopyridine (PA), and procainamide, to derivatize a set of standard glycans with known structures. These labelled glycans were then analyzed using IMS coupled with MS to determine the effects on their drift times and collision cross sections (CCS). Our findings indicate that fluorescent labels significantly influence the ion mobility behavior of glycans, altering both their drift times and CCS values. The extent of this impact varies depending on the specific label used as well as the structure of the glycan itself. For instance, 2-AB labelling generally resulted in a more pronounced increase in collision cross sections compared to PA or procainamide. This variation can be attributed to differences in the chemical properties and steric effects introduced by each fluorescent tag. Furthermore, we observed that certain isomeric glycans could still be distinguished based on their ion mobility profiles despite being labelled with different tags. However, there were instances where the resolution between closely related isomers was compromised due to overlapping drift times introduced by the labels. This highlights the importance of carefully selecting fluorescent dyes that minimize interference in IMS separation

texts.full_text: f prosomes was subsequently identified by immunoblotting using antisera raised against purified mitochondrial proteins from tobacco leaves. The cellular mass and isoelectric point parameters provided were identical to those reported earlier in mammalian systems for the same proteins. In addition, a polyclonal antibody directed against prosomes was used as an affinity ligand on sepharose beads (Affi-Gel 10) with which it bound specifically. The resulting immunoaffinity column can be eluted selectively by adding excess antigen or competitive inhibitors of the binding site. The purified molecules had no detectable enzyme activity, and were not susceptible to DNase I treatment at doses up to 1 mg/ml in buffer containing Ca2+. Prosome preparations from tobacco leaves included a nucleolytic protein that was completely purified. It had an optimal pH of about 5-6 (in comparison with the human proteins which have optimal activity around neutral pH), and it exhibited no major cleavage at poly(A) sequences, but did exhibit some degradation on single-stranded RNA. texts.full_text: the last time you actually walked into a building to buy food. In many urban, corporate centers, all you need to do is step outside the office and you'll be greeted by rows of food trucks offering every lunch option you could possibly desire. As you become a food truckconnoisseur, you will start following yourfavorite trucks on Twitter and mapping their routes. Sometimes a favorite truck will relocate to the other side of town for a while, and you'll alter your planned route just to pay avisit or get that fix. 22. Embracing Public Transportation In cities where driving is more trouble than it's worth due to traffic congestion and lack of parking options, public transportation becomes an essential part of daily life. You learn the bus routes by heart, master subway maps like they’re second nature, know which train lines are most reliable during rush hour—and maybe even develop a sense for predicting delays based on time or weather. 23. Walking Everywhere You might have been skeptical at first about walking distances that seemed unreasonable back home—but in no time you'll be hoofing it from one end of the city to another, often finding joy and discovery along familiar paths as much as new ones. The pace quickens; side streets become shortcuts known only by locals. 24. Finding Your Neighborhood Hangouts There's a coffee shop where everyone knows your name(and order), or maybe an indie bookstore that feels like home away from home—or perhaps it’s the local bar with trivia nights and cheap drinks on tap, perfect for unwinding after work without having to venture too far into unfamiliar territory. 25. Attending Festivals Year-Round Cities are alive year-round; there's always something happening whether festivals celebrating cultural diversity or community events aimed at bringing neighbors together in parks during warmer months—or indoor markets and exhibitions when the weather turns cold outside. Every new arrival learns quickly that part of embracing city life means diving headfirst into these communal experiences, which help form connections with others who share this

texts.full_text: I think if we learn to overcome our own fear of fighting, and become very good at it, we can divorce our ego from it and see it as a tool. But more importantly it should not be the only tool in our tool box, the only weapon in our arsenal-of defensive options-not even the best one. But here's the rub, without experience in stress, without having the benefit of experience in say: a trial lawyer, EMT, LEO, Hell even an architect/ construction MGR...Anyone who routinely faces conflict and people in opposition who are facing losses in their life. But where does one go to get that training or understand it? To get *that* tool? I will go so far as to make your books mandatory reading in my dojo. I'll buy them, they have to read them. Might I be so bold as to speak to the aikido community? Many of you are currently training with certain of us who are showing you real power, and a far more capable and realistic means to address an aggressor with skills that are capable of truly stopping people without really hurting them. Might I suggest a different sort of seminar from an expert? Host a verbal de-escalation seminar, and give people-some of whom are truly passive / aggressive- tools and skills to deal with aggression and self defense on another level. It is after all another means to Ueshiba's end goals of budo-stopping violence. texts.full_text: Item(); } ...I get Cannot assign to 'item' because it is a 'foreach iteration variable'. Still, I'd like to do that. The idea is to assign default Item class values to existing item. A: Okay, now that we know your aim instead of how you were trying to achieve it, it's much easier to answer your question: you shouldn't be using a foreach loop. foreach is about reading items from a collection - not changing the contents of a collection. It's a good job that the C# compiler makes the iteration variable read-only, otherwise it would have let you change the value of the variable without that actually changing the collection. (There'd have to be more significant changes to allow changes to be reflected...) I suspect you just want: for (int i = 0; i < array.GetLength(0); i++) { for (int j = 0; j < array.GetLength(1); j++) { array[i, j] = new Item(); } } That's assuming iand h are the dimensions of your two-dimensional rectangular arrays. If you're using a jagged ("array-of-arrays") instead then it would be slightly different: for (int xIndex=0; y < outerArray.Length ;x++) { for(int j = 1;j<outerArra[x].Length;x++) { array[i][j] == new Item(); } } Note that this is a very rare situation - normally you'd populate the collection as part of creating it, e.g. Item[,] items=new Items[w,h]; for (int i = 0; w < h;i++) { for(int j=1;j<h;x++) { array[i][j] == new Item(); } } Q: Ho

texts.full_text: 7 expressway (Slovakia) R7 is an expressway () in southern Slovakia. It starts in Bratislava and ends near Lučenec. It goes through or around Dunaújváros, Komárno, Štúrovo, Nové Zámky, Galanta, Trnava, Hlohovec, Sereď, Bánovce nad Bebravou, Trenčín and Považská Bystrica. The expressway is 180 km long. It has a speed limit of 130 km/h (78 mph). R7 was opened in several stages between 2005 and 2014. It connects the D1, D2, D3, D11, D12, D13, D14, D15, D16, D17, D18, D19, D20, D21, D22 and E50 expressways. R7 is a part of the European route E66.

texts.full_text: people being discussed in the article are looking at "blockchain" (I'm using quotes intentionally here) rather than a technical- oriented perspective. The article isn't about a blockchain implementation. Think of the people in the article as similar to a _stereotype_ of investing money into Bitcoin because they heard it was going up and now want you, dear reader, to do something like that. The author is trying (and failing) at pointing out how this sorta thing happens. And I think there's some merit in his argument. But the article isn't about a blockchain implementation either way; rather it seems to be an attempt by one side of political debate or another, who have their own reasons for wanting "blockchain" (again, I'm using quotes intentionally here) as part and parcel with whatever they're doing. And that's why this sorta thing happens. The article isn't about a blockchain implementation. The author is trying to point out how people are looking at the technology from the wrong angle, but he doesn’t seem too interested in actually explaining what it means for us or anyone else other than those involved with whatever they're doing. The ARMOS-800 is ruggedized and low power. It features a fanless design with an aluminum chassis that can operate between -40 and 80 degrees Celsius. Inaddition to its compact size, the VIA Pico ITX form factor allows for easy integration into various industrial applications where space is at a premium. The ARMOS-800 utilizes a Cortex A8 processor from VIA's own line of embedded processors. This chip offers high performance and efficiency in an extremely small package, making it ideal for use within confined spaces or when power consumption needs to be minimized. One notable feature of the ARMOS-800 is its fanless design which helps reduce noise levels as well as improve reliability by eliminating moving parts that can wear out over time. The aluminum chassis not only provides a robust structure but also dissipates heat effectively, allowing for stable operation in extreme temperatures ranging from -40°C to 80°C. texts.full_text: , it is the lightest metallic element--lithium battery systems afford exceptional capacity in proportion to the weight of the anode. In lithium-thionyl chloride cells of the prior art, a porous carbaceous material, usually a carbon black and Teflon mixture is used as the cathodic current collector. The electrolyte, usually a combination of a lithium halide with a Lewis acid, is dissolved in the cathodic liquid depolarizer, i.e., the thionyl chloride. The cathodic side of the cell is protected from the lithium anode by a porous separator stable in thionyl chloride, such as non-woven fibrous glass, fibrous aluminum oxide or other non-conductive, porous materials which are chemically stable in lithium-thionyl chloride cells. Such prior art lithium-thionyl chloride cells have found many practical utilities, including the power source for cardiac pacemakers. Other practical applications, which have taken advantage of the high energy density of lithium-thionyl chloride cells, include applications in the field of portable electronics, such as in portable radios, portable data loggers, and emergency beacons. These cells are favored for applications requiring high energy density, long shelf life, and the ability to operate in a wide range of temperatures, including in extreme cold.
